text:
Hey folks :wave: ,
I’m kicking off a new <https://paperswelove.org/|Papers We Love> chapter focused on Computer Science Education in particular. If you haven’t encountered Papers We Love, it’s kinda a book club for academic CS papers. <https://github.com/papers-we-love/edu|This chapter> will be focused on papers about learning and teaching computer science.

Sharing here since I know there’s a lot of interest in the process of learning programming and how our language design and choices influence it. If you have a paper about CS education that you love, or would like to present, we’d love to hear your suggestions! You can submit them via our <https://github.com/papers-we-love/edu/discussions/1|GitHub Discussions board>. Would love to welcome y’all to present!

We are still ramping up, but are targeting the last Thursday of each month for live presentations, with paper discussions continuing async via the discussion board in between.

GitHub Discussions board

We are still ramping up, but are targeting the last Thursday of each month for live presentations, with paper discussions continuing async via the discussion board in between.

text: Got any examples of the sorts of papers you are thinking about? Love the effort. Always wanted a remote first papers we love chapter

elements.text: Got any examples of the sorts of papers you are thinking about? Love the effort. Always wanted a remote first papers we love chapter

text:
<@UK3LH8CF5> aw thanks! There's definitely been a lot of the city chapters that have gone remote, but they're still geo focused. We’re trying here to be explicitly global, remains to be seen how it'll go. 

I think the best way to answer the examples is to talk about the sort of places the papers we're looking at might be submitted. Ones that come to mind are: 
• SIGCSE <https://www.sigcse.org/|https://www.sigcse.org/>
• ITICSE <https://iticse.acm.org/|https://iticse.acm.org/>
• Koli calling <https://www.kolicalling.fi/|https://www.kolicalling.fi/>
Not to say that papers have to be submitted there, but those are some great forums for the topics we’re after

attachments.text: The 21st Koli Calling International Conference on Computing Education Research, November 18-21, 2021
attachments.title: Koli Calling 2021

text: Seems pretty neat! Definitely will try to attend. Sadly, I know nothing about CS education so couldn't propose anything. :slightly_smiling_face:

elements.text: Seems pretty neat! Definitely will try to attend. Sadly, I know nothing about CS education so couldn't propose anything.

text: Computer science and programming doesn't seem to have a popularizer, like many other sciences did: Carl Sagan or Neil de Grasse Tyson for Astrophysics, Steven Pinker for Linguistics (whether you like the person or not), Bertrand Russel for Mathematics and Philosophy, etc. But there is so much fun stuff to tell and show. Does anyone know someone or some place that comes close to such a goal?

elements.text: Computer science and programming doesn't seem to have a popularizer, like many other sciences did: Carl Sagan or Neil de Grasse Tyson for Astrophysics, Steven Pinker for Linguistics (whether you like the person or not), Bertrand Russel for Mathematics and Philosophy, etc. But there is so much fun stuff to tell and show. Does anyone know someone or some place that comes close to such a goal?

text: This is an interesting observation, closest folks I can think of are Tim Berners-Lee, Ted Nelson, Alan Kay, etc. But they're definitely something other than the pop-science, “thought-leader” character and are known for specific work, or ideas.

elements.text: This is an interesting observation, closest folks I can think of are Tim Berners-Lee, Ted Nelson, Alan Kay, etc. But they're definitely something other than the pop-science, “thought-leader” character and are known for specific work, or ideas.

text: Computerphile on YouTube. Same producer or whatever as Numberphile and a couple other channels IIRC. Not terribly famous, but definitely in the space you're looking for.

elements.text: Computerphile on YouTube. Same producer or whatever as Numberphile and a couple other channels IIRC. Not terribly famous, but definitely in the space you're looking for.

text:
IMO there's a distinction between folks like Tyson or Piker, who get lots of speaking events, media coverage and whos income is reliant on maintaining a public presence of some form -> vs people such as Noam Chomsky, or Hawking, who may engage in lots of public discourse but have primarily worked within their fields for most of their lives. 
I've heard people refer to this distinction as one of “thought leader” vs “public intellectual”

I feel there's more of the second category in computing, but they're still relatively niche and I don't know any that regularly engage with society at large.

text:
If you want a charismatic personality, I think Tom Scott could do the job (he does do some CS content on Numberphile and his own channel), but based on his YouTube output he doesn't feel like specializing to that degree.

Edit: Grant Sanderson/3Blue1Brown also occasionally does related work at a very high quality.

text:
It's almost as if there's more a network of popularisers: YouTubers, authors, filmmakers, or public speakers, addressing their own part of a massive space -> the web, ML, game/system design, sociological analysis, interesting mathematics, etc. it's kinda all messy and spread out.

<@U016VUZGUUQ> defo has some good recommendations, I'd add <https://youtube.com/c/K%C3%A1rolyZsolnai|TwoMinutePapers> and <https://youtube.com/c/SebastianLague|Sebastian Lague>, along with all the great smaller creators putting in tons of work to present technical ideas in an engaging (and <https://youtu.be/aVwxzDHniEw|nice-to-look-at>) way.

attachments.text: What a time to be alive!
attachments.title: Two Minute Papers

attachments.text: Hello, on this channel I explore how to create stuff out of code. I hope you find some of it interesting!
attachments.title: Sebastian Lague

text: Thanks for the suggestions so far! I was thinking of Tim Berners-Lee and Alan Kay. Berners-Lee might be recognizable, at least in name, but he is not a great explainer or popularizer. Kay is much better in this regard, but never reached a wider audience. The ones who come to mind are Herbert Simon and Allen Newell, but they went much further than CS. The YouTubers are interesting, and possibly the closest we have in CS to a Carl Sagan, I am afraid.

The YouTubers are interesting, and possibly the closest we have in CS to a Carl Sagan, I am afraid.
